content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 28, 1994

Steven M. De Lucchi
Attorney at Law
36670 Riviera Drive
Fremont, California 94536

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-94-268

Dear Mr. De Lucchi:

This is in response to your letter requesting informal
assistance regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Since your advice request does not refer to a specific
governmental decision but is hypothetical in nature, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to
Regulation 18329(c).²

QUESTIONS

You are a 10-percent owner in a family partnership³ and are
contemplating running for mayor. In the event you are successful
in the mayorai race, you have the following questions:

1. To what extent are you limited in discussing development
of your family's property with city staff and department heads?

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
Regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

3 In a telephone conversation on or about September 22, 1994, you
advised that the 10-percent interest in the family partnership was
your separate property; that the balance of the 90-percent
interest was owned by your parents and your siblings.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-94-268
Page 2


     2. May you sit in the audience while issues concerning your
family's property are being discussed?

     3. May you continue to represent and advocate your family's
interests at public hearings (City Council, Planning Commission
and other city commissions) by speaking at the same podium as
members of the general public?

     4. May you be present at private meetings with city staff
and department heads as the representative and advocate for your
family's interests to discuss plans, submittals, and other matters
that affect the property you and your family own?

     5. If a question arises during the "Oral Communications"
portion of the council meeting (public comments on items not on
the agenda) concerning your family's property, are you permitted
to respond to that question from the city council platform?

     6. May you vote either for or against putting an issue
brought up in question 5⁴ on a future agenda or for or against a
direction to city staff to investigate the matter and report back
to the city council?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1. Regulations 18700 and 18700.1 describe the limitations on
your actions in representing the family partnership before the
city.

     2. Sitting in the audience while issues concerning the
property owned by the family partnership are being discussed does
not constitute making, participating in making, or attempting to
use your official position to influence a governmental decision,
and is not prohibited under the Act.

     3. Since the family partnership is not wholly owned by you
or your immediate family, pursuant to Regulation 18700.1 you may
not appear before the city council and other city commissions to
represent the interests of the family partnership.

     4. You may not be present at private meetings with city
staff and department heads as a representative of the family
partnership since, pursuant to Regulation 18700.1, a public
official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if the official contacts any
member, officer or employee of the agency for the purpose of
influencing the governmental decision.


_______________________________
     ⁴ Although your letter refers to a different question number, the
nature of the question suggests that you meant question 5, and I
have responded accordingly.

File No. I-94-268
Page 3


5. If a question concerning the real property owned by the
family partnership arises during the "Oral Communications"
segment, you may not respond to the question.

6. An official who is disqualified from making a decision on
a particular issue may not vote on a decision to place the issue
on a future agenda or for or against a directive to city staff to
investigate the matter and report back to the city council.

FACTS

You are a 10-percent owner in a family partnership that owns
approximately 2,950 acres of land in Union City, a general law
city with a city manager form of government. The city is in the
process of completing an Area Plan (General Plan Amendment) for
this property and, once completed, will require the processing of
a Specific Plan. Since 1986, you have appeared at all public
hearings to represent and advocate your family's interests in this
property and now you are considering running for the office of
mayor of Union City in 1995.

ANALYSIS

The Act was adopted by the voters in California by initiative
in 1974. The purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from any bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section
81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

A "public official" is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency. If
you are elected mayor of Union City, you will be a "public
official" as defined in the Act. (Section 82048.)

Section 87103 provides in pertinent part:

An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family

File No. I-94-268
Page 4


or on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment
worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest
worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business
on terms available to the public without
regard to official status, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.

                         Section 87103(a)-(d).

You have an investment interest, presumably worth more than
$1,000, in the family partnership. (Section 82034.) In addition,
because of your 10-percent ownership interest in the family
partnership, you have a 10-percent ownership interest in the real
property owned by the family partnership. (Section 82033.)
Finally, as a result of your 10-percent ownership interest in the
family partnership your income includes a pro rata share of income
to the family partnership and, therefore, a source of income to
the family partnership is deemed to be a source of income to you.
(Section 82030.)

Accordingly, if you are elected mayor, you may not make,
participate in making, or in any way attempt to use official
position to influence a governmental decision which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the family
partnership, the real property interest of the family partnership,
and sources of income to the family partnership if your pro rata
share of such income is $250 or more. Assuming such a conflict of
interest exists, you are seeking advice regarding the limitations
placed on your activities in specific situations as a result of
your ownership interest in the family partnership.

Making a Governmental Decision

Subdivision (b) of Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) explains
that a public official makes a governmental decision, when the
official, acting within the authority of his or her office or

File No. I-94-268
Page 5


position, votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, enters into any
contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency, or
determines not to take any of the above actions.

Participating in Making a Governmental Decision

Regulation 18700 further provides in relevant part:

(c) A public official "participates in making
a governmental decision," ... when, acting within
the authority of his or her position, the official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding a governmental decision
...;

* * *

Regulation 18700(c)(1), emphasis added.

Attempting to Use Official Position to Influence a Governmental
Decision

Finally, Regulation 18700.1 states in relevant part:

(a) With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official's agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, "appearances" or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.

(b) Notwithstanding subsection (a) an
official is not attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental
decision of an agency covered by subsection (a) if
the official:

(1) Appears in the same manner as any other
member of the general public before an agency in
the course of its prescribed governmental function
solely to represent himself or herself on a matter
which is related to his or her personal interests.
An official's "personal interests" include, but are
not limited to:

File No. I-94-268
Page 6


(A) An interest in real property which is
wholly owned by the official or members of his or
her immediate family.

(B) A business entity wholly owned by the
official or members of his or her immediate family.

(C) A business entity over which the official
exercises sole direction and control, or over which
the official and his or her spouse jointly exercise
sole direction and control.

*    *    *

Regulation 18700.1(a)-(b), emphasis added.

Thus, if you have a conflict of interest as a result of your
ownership interest in the family partnership, Regulations 18700
and 18700.1 describe the limitations on your actions in
representing the family partnership before the city.

Sitting as a Member of the Audience

Sitting in the audience while issues concerning the property
owned by the family partnership are being discussed does not
constitute making, participating in making, or attempting to use
your official position to influence a governmental decision, as
described in Regulations 18700 and 18700.1, and is not prohibited
under the Act.

Representing the Family Partnership by Speaking at City Council
Meetings as a Member of the General Public or in Private Meetings
with City Staff

Pursuant to Regulation 18700.1, a public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise
attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant
of the agency, including any agency subject to the budgetary
control of his agency.⁵ An exception is created in the statute
where the interest in the real property or in the business entity
is wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate
family.  In such a situation the official may appear in the same
manner as any other member of the public.  Thus, since the family
partnership is not wholly owned by you or your immediate family,

5    In the case of the mayor of the city, planning commissions and
other city commissions are usually under the budgetary control of
the mayor and the city council.

File No. I-94-268
Page 7


you may not appear before the city council and other city
commissions to represent the interests of the family partnership.

     Similarly, you may not be present at private meetings with
city staff and department heads as a representative of the family
partnership since, pursuant to Regulation 18700.1, a public
official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if the official contacts any
member, officer or employee of the agency for the purpose of
influencing the governmental decision.

Questions During Oral Communications Period at City Council
Meetings

     If a question concerning the real property owned by the
family partnership arises during the "Oral Communications"
segment, since the family partnership is not wholly owned by you
or your immediate family, Regulation 18700.1 continues to apply
and you may not respond to the question.6

Voting on a Decision to Place an Issue on the Agenda or to Direct
City Staff to Investigate a Specific Matter

     Pursuant to Regulation 18700(b), generally an official who is
disqualified from making a decision on a particular issue may not
vote on a decision to place the issue on a future agenda or for or
against a directive to city staff to investigate the matter and
report back to the city council.

     If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at 916/322-5901.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jeevan Ahuja
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JA:ak


     6  In any case, since you would have, presumably, disqualified
yourself from making, participating in making or attempting to use
your official position to influence governmental decisions
concerning the real property owned by the family partnership, it
would appear that city officials could answer the question.
